article.title: OpenAI Robotics Chief Caitlin Kalinowski Resigns Over Pentagon Deal, Citing Insufficient Safeguards on Surveillance and Autonomous Weapons
article.summary: Kalinowski becomes the most senior OpenAI employee to publicly oppose the Pentagon contract, warning that domestic surveillance and lethal autonomy deserved more deliberation.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Caitlin Kalinowski, who led robotics and hardware operations at OpenAI, resigned in early March 2026 over the company's agreement to deploy AI models on the Pentagon's classified computing networks. Kalinowski is the most senior OpenAI employee to publicly oppose the deal, which was signed on February 27 and drew immediate criticism for its timing and rollout.

Her departure adds an internal dimension to a controversy that has already triggered a consumer boycott, a surge in ChatGPT uninstalls, and an open letter from hundreds of AI industry employees.

## What Happened

Kalinowski, who joined OpenAI in November 2024 after leading Meta's Orion AR glasses project and spending nearly nine years developing VR headsets at Oculus, announced her resignation on social media. She framed the decision as one of principle rather than personal conflict.

"Surveillance of Americans without judicial oversight and lethal autonomy without human authorization are lines that deserved more deliberation than they got," Kalinowski wrote, according to [NPR](https://www.npr.org/2026/03/08/nx-s1-5741779/openai-resigns-ai-pentagon-guardrails-military). She added that policy guardrails around certain AI uses were not sufficiently defined before OpenAI announced the agreement.

In a separate statement reported by [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/openai-hardware-chief-resigns), Kalinowski described the issue as "a governance concern first and foremost," arguing that "these are too important for deals or announcements to be rushed."

She retained "deep respect" for CEO Sam Altman and said the decision was "about principle, not people," as reported by [Computerworld](https://www.computerworld.com/article/4142366/openai-robotics-chief-quits-over-pentagon-deal.html). Kalinowski indicated she plans to continue working on "responsible physical AI."

## The Pentagon Deal

OpenAI signed the agreement with the Department of Defense on February 27, making its AI models available inside secure government computing systems. The deal was announced the same day the Pentagon designated rival Anthropic a supply-chain risk over its refusal to allow its models to be used for domestic mass surveillance or fully autonomous weapons, as reported by [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/03/07/openai-robotics-leader-caitlin-kalinowski-resignation-pentagon-surveillance-autonomous-weapons-anthropic/).

The timing drew sharp criticism. By the following Monday, Altman conceded on social media that the rollout had been mishandled, calling it "opportunistic and sloppy," according to [Computerworld](https://www.computerworld.com/article/4142366/openai-robotics-chief-quits-over-pentagon-deal.html).

OpenAI subsequently revised the agreement to include explicit prohibitions on domestic surveillance of U.S. persons. The company stated that the partnership "creates a workable path for responsible national security uses of AI while making clear our red lines: no domestic surveillance and no autonomous weapons," as reported by [NPR](https://www.npr.org/2026/03/08/nx-s1-5741779/openai-resigns-ai-pentagon-guardrails-military). OpenAI also said it would deploy models on cloud networks only, develop a "safety stack" to prevent misuse, and ensure that the government cannot override model refusals, according to [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/openai-hardware-chief-resigns).

## What We Don't Know

Several questions remain unanswered. The specific technical safeguards OpenAI describes as its "safety stack" have not been publicly detailed. It is unclear whether additional OpenAI employees are considering departures over the deal, though Kalinowski joined hundreds of OpenAI and Google employees who signed an open letter opposing AI use for mass surveillance and autonomous weapons, according to [Computerworld](https://www.computerworld.com/article/4142366/openai-robotics-chief-quits-over-pentagon-deal.html).

The enforceability of OpenAI's stated red lines also remains an open question. The company says the government agreed to its safeguards, but the terms of the classified agreement have not been made public, and it is not clear what mechanisms exist to verify compliance.

## Broader Context

Kalinowski's resignation is the latest in a series of high-profile departures and internal conflicts at OpenAI over the company's direction. The Pentagon deal has already triggered a consumer backlash, with ChatGPT uninstalls surging 295 percent in the days following the announcement.

The episode also highlights a deepening divide within the AI industry over military applications. Anthropic's refusal to remove guardrails on surveillance and autonomous weapons led to its blacklisting by the Trump administration, while OpenAI's willingness to negotiate drew both a lucrative contract and significant reputational damage. As AI companies face increasing pressure from both government clients and their own workforces, the question of where to draw ethical lines on military use shows no sign of resolution.
